description: A 'porewi' is an effect attributed to an intervention but outside its stated target. It may be helpful, neutral, troublesome, or harmful, so the word does not minimize a burden simply by placing it to the side. 'whekaro' (adverse effect) selects the harmful or burdensome cases.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into true-mid rounded back 'o'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and move into stressed true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then move the tongue forward and unround into high-front 'i'.
usage_notes: Separate effects are countable; uncounted use keeps one in view as a condition. The account identifies the intervention and timing, then describes the observed effect and burden. Causal evidence remains open. Exact product safety terminology stays with its source when the formal category matters.

examples.translation: The medicine has a side effect.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A neutral additional-effect term lets benefit and burden be reported without presuming either harmlessness or cause.

semantic_domains.cognition: an attributed additional effect with stated uncertainty
semantic_domains.physical: an effect beyond an intervention's stated target
